content.full_text:
RE
Fern PolrrrcAl Pnecrrcps C oMMrssroN
,128 J Street r Suire 620 o Sacramento. CA 95811-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
January 3, 2008
Elizabeth Martyn
McCormick, Kidman & Behrens, LLP
650 Town Center Drive, Suite 100
Costa Mesa, CA 92626-7 17 8
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-07-193
Dear Ms. Martyn:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Canyon Lake City
Councilmember Mary Craton regarding her duties under the conflict-of-inteiest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Because you do not seek advice
regarding a specific governmental decision, we can provide you only informal assistance.
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an
opinion or formal written advice. (Section 831 14; Regulation I832gG), copy enilosed.)
Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act.
l^qd]li9nallYl the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation
18329(b)(8)(A), copy enclosed.) Therefore, nothing in this letter evaluates any conduct
that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this lettei apply only
to prospective actions.
QUESTION
May Councilmember Craton participate in decisions regarding the survey and
plan area regarding the Sky Blue development (..Sky Blue")?
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulationi of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l I0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the calitbrnia
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
ri;i;l


File No. l-07-193
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
Based on the facts you have provided, the Councilmember's real property interest
is deemed indirectly involved. Therefore, the financial effect of the govemmental
decision on the real property is presumed not to be material and, assuming the
presumption is correct, she may participate in these decisions. However, iven if the
councilmember has a disqualifying conflict of interest in the Sky Blue decisions, your
facts indicate that the public generally exception for small jurisdictions may appty. lSee
discussion below.)
FACTS
Mary Craton is a member of the Canyon Lake City Council. Her personal
residence is located more than 500 feet from the boundaries of the proporid Sky Blue
development. The councilmember does not have any other property interests within this
area.
The City of Canyon Lake is has a population of 30,000 or less and covers a
geographic area of fewer than 10 square miles. Ms. Craton is required to live in the city
and was elected at-large to the city council. Her personal residence is located on a lot
that is smaller than a quarter acre, which is generally the same size as the medium lot size
within the city. There are at least 20 other properties (owned separately) within 500 feet
of the boundaries of the survey area and the Sky Blue developmint thai are "substantially
similar in value."2
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused bytheir own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87 100 provides:
"No public official at any level of state or local govemment shall make,
participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest."
The Commission has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a "financial interest" in a decision. (Regulation tgzoo(u).)
' Ms. Kara Germane of your firm stated during a phone conversation on December 2g, ZOO1 that
the 20 other properties within 500 feet of the boundaries of the survey area and the Sky Blue development
are "substantially similar in value." She said the properties were "track homes" and some were different
models, but that all were similar in value.


File No. l-07-193
Page No. 3
Steps I and 2. Is councilmember Craton a "public official" within the
meaning of Section 87100 and will she be making, participating in making or
influencing a governmental decision?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee, or
consultant of a state or local government agency." As a member of the Canyon Lake
City Council (a local government agency), she is a public official under the Act.
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contrictual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 15702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 157023.)
Therefore, councilmember Craton may not make, participate in making, or
otherwise use her position to influence any decision involving the Sky Blue development
that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of her economic
interests.
step 3. what are councilmember crayton's economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain enumerated economic interests. (See Section 87103 and Regulations 1g703-
18703.5.)
o A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of 92,000 or more. (Section g7103(a);
Regulation I 8703. 1(a).)
o A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she is
a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or hords any position of
management. (Secrion 87103(d); Regularion 18703.1(b).)
o A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section g7103(b); Regulation
18703.2.)


File No. l-07-193
Page No. 4
o An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, totaling $500 or more within t2 months prior to the decision. lSection
87 103(c); Regulation 1 8703.3.)
o A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts total $390 or more within the 12 months preceding the decision. (Section
87 103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)
o A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. (Section
87 103; Regulation 1 8703.5.)
Based on the facts submitted, the only economic interest indicated is
Councilmember Craton's economic interest in her personal residence. Because you have
not provided any facts indicating that any other economic interest may be affected, our
analysis is limited to Councilmember Craton's economic interest in her real property.3
Step 4. Will Councilmember Craton's economic interests be directly or
indirectly involved in decisions?
Real property that is not the subject of a govemmental decision is directly
involved in the decision, if any part of the real property is located within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property that is the subject of the
governmental decision. (Regulati on I87 04.Z(aX I ).)
You state that Councilmember Craton's personal residence is located more than
500 feet outside the boundaries of the Sky Blue development. Therefore, this property is
not directly involved in the governmental decisions you have described. Real property
that is not directly involved in a given decision is indirectly involved in that detision.
(Regulations 187 04.2(dX2) and I 8705.2(b).)
Steps 5 and 6. What is the applicable materiality standard and is it
reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decision upon
Councilmember Craton's economic interest will meet this materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a govemmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) An effect on economic interests is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
I In addition, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financialeffecti" rule (Section g7103;
regulation 18703.5). Because you did not provide any facts to suggest that this economic interest is
implicated, we do not include it in our analysis.


File No. l-07-193
Page No. 5
possibility. (ln re Thorner (I975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) What makes the financial effect
"substantially likely" is ultimately dependent on the facts surrounding a specific decision.
Ap p I ic ab I e M at e ri al i ty S tand ard and F o re s e e ab il ity
For property indirectly involved in a governmental decision, any financial effect
of the decision on the property is presumed. not to be material. (Regulaiion
18705.2(b)(l).) Because the councilmember's property is located more than 500 feet
from the boundaries of the proposed Sky Blue development, it is presumed that the
governmental decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
her property.
However, this presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific
circumstances regarding the govemmental decision, its financial effect, anditre nature of
the real property in which the public official has an economic interest that make it
reasonably foreseeable the decision will have a material financial effect on the real
property in which the official has an interest. Examples of these specific circumstances
include circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
"(c) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels,
air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood." (Regulation
1 870s.2(b)(2xA)_(C). )
In prior advice, we stated the materiality presumption may have been rebutted
where a councilmember owned property located more tlian 500 fiet from a street
extension project but the project, if approved, foreseeably altered traffic pattems and
property values. (Thorson Advice Letter, No. 4-04-238, enclosed.) In another case, the
governmental decision involving a development project that was one-quarter mile from
an official's residence appeared to change the character of the area near the official's
home from largely rural to largely residential. (HerrickAdvice Letter, No. 4-04-019.)
Councilmember Craton will need to determine whether the Sky Blue project will
have a material financial effect on her real property based upon any of the above factors,
or any additional factors that could affect the value of her real property. you did not
provide any details about potential impacts on surrounding areas as a result of the
proposed Sky Blue development. However, the Sky Blue development appears to be a
significant, large-scale project that may possibly rebut the above pprurnption due to its


File No. I-07-193
Page No. 6
potential financial effects and its impact on the character of the surrounding areas,
including Ms. Craton's neighborhood.
Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (ln re
Oglesby, (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71, 77.), it is ultimately up to Councilmembei Craton to
determine whether her facts are sufficient to rebut this presumption.
steps 7 and 8. The "public generally" and,.legally required participation,
exceptions.
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the
governmental decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from
its effect on the public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)),
or if the official is legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18708).
You have not presented any facts indicating that the legally required participation
exception could apply to Councilmember Craton's situation.
However, you included facts to suggest that the public generally exception under
Regulation 18707.10 may apply.
Public Generally, Small lurisdictions: Regulation 1g707.10 (which became
operative on January 13,2007) is an exception to the conflict of interest rules under the
Act which public officials in small jurisdictions may use. If a public official is found to
have a conflict of interest under the first six steps of the eight-step analysis, but meets all
six requirements under 18707.10(a), that official may still participate in the governmental
decision(s) at issue.
Those six requirements are:
"(l) The jurisdiction of the public official's agency has a population of
30,000 or less and covers a geographic area of ten square miles or less;
"(2)The public official is required to live within the jurisdiction;
"(3) The public official, if elected, has been elected in an at-large
jurisdiction;
"(4) The official's property is more than 300 feet from the boundaries of
the property that is the subject of the govemmental decision;
"(5) The official's property is located on a lot not more than one-quarter
acre in size or not larger than 125 percent of the median residentiil lot
size for the jurisdiction; and


File No. I-07-193
Page No. 7
"(6) There are at least 20 other properties under separate ownership
within a 500 foot radius of the boundaries of the property that is the
subject of the governmental decision that are similar in value."
Assuming for pulposes of this analysis that Councilmember Craton has a
disqualifying conflict of interest, the above exception applies to her personal residence
because your facts indicate that all of the factors under Regulation 18707.10 are met.
Therefore, even if Councilmember Craton determines she has a disqualifying
conflict of interest, the public generally exception would apply, and she may nonetheless
participate in decisions involving the Sky Blue development.
lf you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Commission Counsel,
Legal Division
Enclosure
ERjgl
